facts.text: Highland Landing was a bustling 19th‑century commercial hub with factories, warehouses, and ferry operations that served regional trade until railroads isolated the waterfront.
The Brown–Ellis House, built around 1800 as a farmhouse and expanded 1835 in Greek Revival style, stands as a key historic landmark in Highland.
The Anthony Yelverton House, erected circa 1754 by settler Anthony Yelverton, is one of Highland’s oldest surviving structures and marks early colonial settlement along the Hudson.
In 2023, Highland preserved a historic burial ground for enslaved individuals near the Yelverton House, acknowledging the role of enslaved labor in early settlement.
Highland occupies about 13.99 km² of rolling Hudson River land, with elevations rising to Illinois Mountain, the highest point along the river in Ulster County at 1,125 feet.
